question_text: Water at 100°C is boiling in a beaker over a Bunsen burner. The mass of water decreases from 250g to 150g in 10 minutes. The specific latent heat of vaporisation of water is 2,260,000 J/kg. Calculate the energy needed to evaporate the lost mass of water, and explain why the temperature does not increase above 100°C even though the burner continues heating.
correct_answer: Mass evaporated = 250g - 150g = 100g = 0.1kg. Energy = mass × specific latent heat = 0.1 × 2,260,000 = 226,000J. Temperature stays at 100°C because the heat energy is used to convert liquid water into gas (breaking particle bonds) rather than increasing particle kinetic energy.
explanation: During boiling, added heat energy breaks bonds to change the state, not increase temperature. This is why water stays at 100°C while boiling—once all liquid becomes gas, temperature could increase further if heating continues.
